danger_signals.visual_form: Grotesque, oversized creature designs with exaggerated anatomical features (multiple eyes, parasitic blobs, fetus-like tongues, exposed brains)
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate threat escalation; player encounters extreme danger requiring grapple-based combat

danger_signals.visual_form: Non-humanoid, biomechanical, or organic-horror aesthetics (flying cyclops heads, sentient mouths, burrowing worms, jellyfish-like entities)
danger_signals.meaning: Global existential threat; enemy is foreign/otherworldly and conquers Earth

danger_signals.visual_form: Smaller variants and minion creatures throughout scrolling level stages (sand worms, exploding urchins, flying blade rays)
danger_signals.meaning: Persistent environmental danger; wave-based threat attrition

danger_signals.visual_form: Sand pit traps created by Gapurin burrowing; lava zone stage setting
danger_signals.meaning: Environmental damage source; stage-specific mechanical threats beyond combat

safety_signals.visual_form: Three distinct evolved monster designs (Super Geon dragon-form, Cyber Woo cyborg, Atomic Guy powered costume)
safety_signals.meaning: Player agency; mutated allies represent Earth's defensive advantage against alien threat

safety_signals.visual_form: Geographic stage transitions (American City → French City → Grand Canyon → Desert → Sea Bed → Lava Zone → Enemy Hideout)
safety_signals.meaning: Territorial control narrative; player advances through defined safe zones toward alien source

safety_signals.visual_form: Post-level scoring feedback (Good/Very Good/Nice Going/That's Strong/Congratulations/Great/Miraculous)
safety_signals.meaning: Player competence signal; ranking reflects combat mastery and suggests survivability

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Playable monster designs mutated/upgraded from previous conflicts (Super Geon, Cyber Woo, Atomic Guy); mechanical/biological enhancement markers
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Appear in global cities; defend Earth locations (American City, French City, Grand Canyon, Desert, Sea Bed, Lava Zone before final hideout)

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: King Famardy (final boss) as faction leader; hierarchical boss structure (Huge Frogger → Eifflelyte → Clawhead → Beetle Master → Aqua Slug → Lavicus); minion wave creatures (I.T.O, Kanako, P-Head, Pujimond)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Culminates in Enemy Hideout final stage; pervasive across all geographic locations representing global conquest attempt

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Post-massacre recovery (three years after King of the Monsters 1)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Surviving monsters appear mutated, refurbished, powered-up with new forms and abilities
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player selects evolved fighter; monstersact as Earth's evolved defense capability

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Alien boss monsters occupy Earth cities in sequence; environmental stages show contamination (lava zone, underwater sea bed)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Mandatory boss battles after each level; minion waves throughout stages indicate occupying force presence

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: King Famardy visual design (massive blob with multiple eyes and fetus-like tongue) represents ultimate threat form
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Final stage requires defeating Famardy clone, then destroying remnants under time pressure; determines ending quality (good vs bad ending)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Boss monster designs escalate in grotesqueness and biological horror (frog → giant with parasite → two-headed → insectoid brain dome → aquatic slug → gremlin with egg nozzle → ultimate blob)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Button-mashing grapple mechanics for boss encounters; minion encounter density increases in later stages

environmental_storytelling.example: Stages progress from American City through French landmarks, natural wonders (Grand Canyon), desert, oceanic depths, volcanic zones to alien hideout—visual path shows alien infiltration across global civilization

environmental_storytelling.technique: Monster design language communicates threat origin
environmental_storytelling.example: Alien minions use non-Earth anatomies (cyclops heads, flying mouths, burrowing pods, jellyfish forms) visually distinguish them from previously-encountered Earth monsters

Aquatic stage contains water-dwelling monsters (Aqua Slug boss, Exploding Urchin, Blade Ray); Lava Zone contains volcanic-adapted creatures; desert stage features burrowing enemies—visual ecology signals enemy adaptation

environmental_storytelling.technique: Boss monster naming variants signal cultural appropriation
environmental_storytelling.example: Japanese names (Horn du Out, Yam a Mordon) vs Western names (Eifflelyte, Clawhead)—visual-linguistic shift suggests alien contamination of human cultural signification

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Takes offensive role in combat; dragon-like mutation suggests increased power and transformation from original form
Mechanical enhancement suggests technological arms race response to alien threat; Mechani-Kong resemblance signals mechanical superiority attempt
Costume change indicates preparation/readiness state; 'more powered up' suggests escalated capabilities for global threat
Appears as ultimate boss after all regional bosses defeated; possesses multiple eyes suggesting omniscience/surveillance; fetus-like tongue suggests parasitic/reproductive threat
Spawns throughout levels; varied morphologies (flying, burrowing, explosive) indicate diverse invasion tactics; minion density suggests occupying force saturation
Lays eggs that hatch into minion monsters; egg-nozzle apparatus signals breeding/proliferation threat—visual indicator of invasion sustainability mechanism

notes: King of the Monsters 2 Genesis version significantly differs from original arcade beat-em-up; Genesis port transforms it into one-on-one fighting game with playable bosses. Visual communication relies on monster design grotesqueness and anatomical horror to signal alien threat. Geographic stage progression creates territorial conflict narrative. Post-game ending (good/bad) determined by time-limited destruction mechanic signals world-state consequence. Limited color-identity and symbol documentation in source material; faction distinction primarily through creature design language and stage occupation patterns.
